questions.question: Why is memory coherence time critical for first-generation repeaters?

options.text: To store entanglement while waiting for other segments

questions.explanation: First-generation repeaters must store entanglement from early successful segments while waiting for later segments to succeed. If coherence time is shorter than this wait, the stored entanglement decays and the protocol fails.

questions.question: What is memory multiplexing?

options.text: Using multiple memories to store classical data
Storing multiple quantum states in parallel modes

questions.explanation: Multiplexing uses multiple temporal, spectral, or spatial modes to attempt entanglement generation in parallel. If any mode succeeds, that entanglement is used, dramatically improving effective success rates.

questions.question: Which memory platform offers the longest coherence times?
questions.explanation: Trapped ions can achieve coherence times of seconds to minutes, far exceeding other platforms. This makes them excellent for first-generation repeaters where long storage is critical.

questions.question: Why is telecom wavelength (1550 nm) important for memories?

options.text: Fiber has minimum loss at this wavelength
Quantum effects are strongest at 1550 nm

questions.explanation: Telecom fiber has minimum loss (~0.2 dB/km) at 1550 nm. Memories operating at this wavelength avoid the need for frequency conversion, which introduces additional losses and complexity.

questions.question: How does multiplexing with N modes improve success rate?
questions.explanation: With N modes each having success probability p, the probability of at least one success is 1-(1-p)^N. For small p, this approaches N×p. With 100 modes and p=1%, success becomes ~63%.

questions.question: What memory requirements are relaxed in third-generation repeaters?
questions.explanation: Third-generation repeaters use continuous quantum error correction that actively protects coherence. This relaxes the natural coherence time requirement, as the QEC code corrects decoherence errors.

questions.question: Higher memory efficiency always compensates for shorter coherence time.
questions.explanation: False. While efficiency affects success probability, coherence time determines whether stored entanglement remains useful. If coherence is too short, even perfect efficiency cannot help - the entanglement decays before it can be used.
